name: Almadén Circular via Saceruela and Puebla de
name_original: Puebla de Don Rodrigo desde Almadén – circular por Saceruela y Valdemanco del Esteras
description: This difficult 87.6-mile (141.0 km) road cycling route from Almadén offers challenging climbs and scenic views of the Montes de Toledo.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Almadén traverse a landscape characterized by rolling hills and open rural areas, offering varied terrain for cyclists. The region features a mix of agricultural lands and historical mining areas, providing unique visual interest along the routes. Elevations are generally moderate, with some challenging climbs present, particularly on longer routes. The area's geology contributes to a diverse cycling experience, moving through different natural and historical settings. Best road cycling routes around Almadén The most popular road cycling route is Almadén Circular via Saceruela and Puebla de, a 87.6 miles (140.9 km) trail that takes 6 hours 34 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through rural landscapes. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Almadenejos, Saceruela, and Abenójar Loop, a difficult 62.2 miles (100.0 km) path. This route offers expansive views of the countryside and passes through several small towns. Local road cyclists also love the Almadén Bullring – Almadén Mining Park loop from Almadén, a 13.8 miles (22.2 km) trail leading through areas of historical mining interest, often completed in about 1 hour 13 minutes. Road cycling around Almadén is defined by rolling hills, open rural landscapes, and historical mining heritage. The network offers options for both moderate and challenging ability levels, with several long distance road cycling routes near Almadén. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 30 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Almadén's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Almadén?
A: There are 6 road cycling routes around Almadén documented on komoot, offering a range of distances and difficulties. These routes traverse the region's characteristic rolling hills and open rural areas.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes for beginners in Almadén?
A: While most routes in Almadén are rated as 'difficult' due to elevation gain and distance, there is one moderate option: the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38158596" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén Bullring – Almadén Mining Park loop from Almadén&lt;/a&gt;. This route is 13.8 miles (22.2 km) long and takes about 1 hour 13 minutes, making it a good choice for those looking for a less strenuous ride.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes around Almadén?
A: For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, Almadén offers several difficult routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1067787643/almaden-circular-via-saceruela-and-puebla-de" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén Circular via Saceruela and Puebla de&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 87.6 miles (140.9 km) route with significant elevation gain. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1086174571/almadenejos-saceruela-and-abenojar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadenejos, Saceruela, and Abenójar Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 62.2 miles (100.0 km) with expansive countryside views.

Q: Can I find long-distance road cycling routes in the Almadén area?
A: Yes, Almadén is well-suited for long-distance road cycling. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1067787643/almaden-circular-via-saceruela-and-puebla-de" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén Circular via Saceruela and Puebla de&lt;/a&gt; is the longest at 87.6 miles (140.9 km). Other substantial routes include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1086174571/almadenejos-saceruela-and-abenojar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadenejos, Saceruela, and Abenójar Loop&lt;/a&gt; (62.2 miles / 100.0 km) and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24527466" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Miner's Monument – Almadén Bullring loop from Almadén&lt;/a&gt; (51.9 miles / 83.5 km).

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Almadén?
A: The best time for road cycling in Almadén is typically spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October) when temperatures are milder. Summers can be very hot, and while winters are generally mild, some routes might be cooler or experience occasional rain.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes around Almadén?
A: Road cycling routes around Almadén feature a landscape of rolling hills and open rural areas. You'll encounter a mix of agricultural lands and historical mining areas, providing varied visual interest. Elevations are generally moderate, but some routes include challenging climbs, offering a diverse cycling experience.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural attractions to see along the road cycling routes in Almadén?
A: Absolutely. Almadén is rich in history, particularly its mining heritage. Routes often pass by or near significant sites such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3901719" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén Mining Park&lt;/a&gt;, the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5620080" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén Bullring&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3901752" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Miners' Museum&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1085208308/almaden-mercury-and-castles-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén Mercury and Castles Route&lt;/a&gt; specifically highlights this historical aspect.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Almadén?
A: Most road cycling routes in Almadén start and end in or near the town center, or in smaller villages along the way. You can typically find public parking available in these areas. It's advisable to check local parking regulations upon arrival, especially in town centers.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the road cycling routes?
A: Many routes in Almadén pass through small towns and villages where you can find local cafes, bars, or small shops to refuel. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1067787643/almaden-circular-via-saceruela-and-puebla-de" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén Circular via Saceruela and Puebla de&lt;/a&gt; passes through Saceruela and Puebla de Don Rodrigo, offering opportunities for a break. Always carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer routes, as services may be sparse between villages.

Q: What makes road cycling in Almadén unique?
A: Road cycling in Almadén offers a unique blend of natural beauty and historical significance. The routes wind through rolling hills and agricultural landscapes, punctuated by the striking visual interest of its historical mining areas. This combination provides a distinct experience, different from many other cycling regions.

Q: Are there any viewpoints or particularly scenic spots on the road cycling routes?
A: The open rural landscapes and rolling hills of Almadén naturally offer numerous scenic vistas. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1086174571/almadenejos-saceruela-and-abenojar-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadenejos, Saceruela, and Abenójar Loop&lt;/a&gt; provide expansive views of the countryside. The varied terrain, moving through different natural and historical settings, ensures a constantly changing and picturesque backdrop for your ride.

Q: Are the road cycling routes in Almadén suitable for families?
A: Given that most road cycling routes in Almadén are rated as 'difficult' or 'moderate' with significant distances and elevation gains, they may not be ideal for young children or inexperienced family members. The shorter, moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38158596" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almadén Bullring – Almadén Mining Park loop from Almadén&lt;/a&gt; could be an option for families with older, experienced cyclists.

Q: What are the typical weather conditions for road cycling in Almadén?
A: Almadén experiences a Mediterranean climate with hot, dry summers and mild winters. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures, making them ideal for cycling. During summer, it's crucial to start early to avoid the midday heat and carry plenty of water. Winters are generally mild, but occasional rain or cooler temperatures can occur.
tips: World Heritage Site since 2012. Also called Plaza Nueva, whose construction was carried out between 1752 and 1754, it has a hexagonal shape and two stories high. It is made up of a single block, made up of twenty-four exterior homes, in whose interior courtyard the square itself was designed.

Saceruela is a small rural town next to the CM-415 road. Rest in the Nuestra Señora de las Cruces Parish located in the Plaza de la Constitución. The town is one of the starting points of the historic route of the Camino de Guadalupe de Levante that connects the Iberian Peninsula with the town of Guadalupe in Cáceres.

Municipality located in the valley of the Tierteafuera river. In the middle of the Guadiana union basin, framed and partially divided by the Grande and Chico streams, 2 km from the Guadiana river course.
There is evidence of habitation in this term since pre-Roman times, as evidenced by the presence of several castellars, such as the one in the Castle or the one in Castilnegro, and even a motilla found in the 70s and located at the exit of the town .
Beautiful town that is located at the foot of a small hill called Pinguta del Morro and that is also located south of the Río Siruela.

Valdemanco del Esteras is a Spanish municipality in the province of Ciudad Real, in the autonomous community of Castilla-La Mancha, however, the town is located on a beautiful road where the province of Badajoz begins.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Puebla de Don Rodrigo from Almadén circular cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as difficult for road cycling. The Montes de Toledo region, where the route is located, features a mountainous and hilly landscape with significant climbs and descents. Cyclists should expect varied terrain and demanding ascents, requiring a good level of physical fitness.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain and scenery can I expect on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: You can expect a challenging and varied terrain with undulating roads, significant climbs, and descents. The scenery is renowned for its natural beauty, offering picturesque views of rural landscapes, rivers, and dense forests. You'll pass through areas described as a 'Wild West' landscape with junipers, strawberry trees, holm oaks, and diverse wildlife. Roads often have little traffic, allowing for an undisturbed experience.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or towns along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes through several interesting points. You'll start near the historic Almadén Bullring. The route also takes you through the settlements of Saceruela, Puebla de Don Rodrigo, Tamurejo, and Valdemanco del Esteras, offering glimpses into traditional Spanish village life.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Considering the challenging nature and the climate of Castilla-La Mancha, the best times to cycle this route are typically spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October). During these seasons, temperatures are generally milder and more favorable for long-distance cycling than the intense heat of summer or the cooler, potentially wetter conditions of winter.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking for this exact route isn't detailed, Almadén, being a larger town, would be the most practical starting point. Look for public parking areas within Almadén, especially near the Almadén Bullring or other central locations, where you can safely leave your vehicle.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for road cycling on public roads in this region of Castilla-La Mancha. You can enjoy the route freely.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: Given its difficult classification, significant distance (over 140 km), and substantial elevation gain (1500m), this route is not recommended for families with young children or beginner cyclists. It requires a high level of fitness and experience with challenging road cycling.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes, restaurants, or accommodation options along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through several towns and settlements like Saceruela, Puebla de Don Rodrigo, and Valdemanco del Esteras, you should find local cafes, restaurants, and potentially small guesthouses or accommodation options for refreshments and breaks. It's advisable to check opening hours, especially in smaller villages.

faqs.question: Is the Puebla de Don Rodrigo from Almadén circular route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As this is a long-distance road cycling route, bringing a dog along for the ride itself is generally not practical or safe. While the towns you pass through may have dog-friendly areas, the cycling portion is not designed for accompanying pets. If you plan to stay overnight, you would need to research dog-friendly accommodation in the specific towns.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, while primarily a road cycling route, it briefly intersects with sections of the Camino Natural del Guadiana (3% of the route) and the Sendero Pantano-Charco Las Parras. Campo de Aviación (2% of the route). These are mostly short overlaps.

faqs.question: What should I pack for a day of cycling on this route?
faqs.answer: For this challenging and long route, pack essentials like sufficient water and nutrition, a repair kit for your bike, appropriate cycling attire for varied weather, sun protection, a first-aid kit, and a fully charged phone. Given the remote sections, consider carrying extra layers and being prepared for self-sufficiency between towns.
